generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kumta]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-KA-023]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Naik]] | DOB [[DOB|1998-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|26]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Bus Driver]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-004]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Bhat]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Naik]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9886543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-1108]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Uttara Kannada]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough with yellowish sputum for the past 10 days, associated with low-grade fever and generalized body ache.

History of present illness: The patient reports a productive cough that started 10 days ago, initially dry but now producing approximately 50ml of yellow-green sputum daily. He has experienced fever in the evenings, peaking at 100.8°F, which subsides with paracetamol. He also complains of diffuse myalgia and fatigue, making it difficult to perform his duties as a bus driver. He denies any shortness of breath at rest but notes mild breathlessness on exertion. He has been taking over-the-counter cough syrups without significant relief.

Examination:
General: Patient is a well-built, moderately built male, appearing ill. No cyanosis or clubbing.
Vital signs: Temperature: 100.4°F, Pulse: 98/min, Respiratory rate: 20/min, Blood pressure: 120/80 mmHg, SpO2: 96% on room air.
Respiratory: Trachea central. Chest expansion is symmetrical. Percussion note is dull in the right lower zone. Breath sounds are vesicular with coarse crackles heard in the right infrascapular area. No wheeze.
Cardiovascular: S1 and S2 are audible, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray (PA view): Shows an opacity with air-fluid level in the right lower lobe, suggestive of lung abscess.
Complete Blood Count: Hemoglobin: 13.5 g/dL, Total WBC count: 12,500/cmm with neutrophilia (85%).
Sputum for Gram stain and culture: Pending.

Diagnosis:
1. Right lower lobe lung abscess
2. Community-acquired pneumonia

Treatment plan:
1. Admit the patient for intravenous antibiotics. Start empirical therapy with IV Ceftriaxone 1g once daily and IV Metronidazole 500mg three times daily.
2. Obtain sputum culture and sensitivity to tailor antibiotic therapy.
3. Perform a CT scan of the chest to better delineate the abscess cavity.
4. Consult with the Pulmonology department for possible interventional drainage if the patient does not show clinical improvement in 48-72 hours.
5. Provide supportive care including antipyretics for fever and adequate hydration.
6. Advise the patient to avoid driving until he is clinically stable.

Follow-up: Review in 3 days or sooner if symptoms worsen.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Uttara Kannada.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Konkani (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 26
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Driver, Bus
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Uttara Kannada
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Basavaraj Gorabaal is a 26-year-old male bus driver in rural Uttara Kannada, Karnataka, with higher secondary education and a never-married status. He is fluent in Konkani and Kannada, and his lifestyle includes disciplined vehicle maintenance and a habit of checking fuel gauges obsessively. His cultural background is rooted in a rural coastal community with traditional Hindu customs.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
शस्त्रक्रिया नोंद — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital, Kumta]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] वॉर्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Basavaraj Gorabaal]] [[AGE|26]] [[GENDER|Male]] सर्जन [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Deshpande]]
प्रक्रिया / निष्कर्ष: दुयेंती कडेन पॉट्स स्पाइनचो एक गुंतागुंतीचो प्रकार आशिल्लो, जातूंत थोरॅसिक मणक्यांचो आस्पाव आशिल्लो, हें फुफ्फुसा भायल्या क्षयरोगाचें एक रूप आशिल्लें आनी ताका वांगडाच टाईप 2 मधुमेह हो एक बिगर-संसर्गजन्य दुयेंसाचो सह-व्याधी आशिल्लो. शस्त्रक्रिये पयलींच्या तपासणेंत सी.टी. स्कॅन केलो, जातूंत T8-T9 कडेन एक व्हड पॅराव्हर्टेब्रल गळू (abscess) आनी मणक्याच्या कुडीचो नाश जाल्ल्याचें दिसलें. दुयेंतीक जनरल ॲनेस्थेसिया सकयल दवरलो. फाटल्या मदल्या भागांतल्यान (posterior midline) मार्ग आपणायलो. शस्त्रक्रिये वेळार, थंय व्हड प्रमाणांत ग्रॅन्युलेशन उती (granulation tissue) आनी केसिएटिंग पदार्थ मेळ्ळे. गळू (abscess) निथळ केलो आनी तातूंतलो कचरो काडून उडयलो. मणक्याच्या दोरीचें (spinal cord) दडपण उणें करपा खातीर T8 आनी T9 मणक्याच्या कुडींचो कांय भाग काडून उडयलो. संरचनेत आदार दिवपा खातीर टायटॅनियम मेश केज घालो आनी T7 ते T10 मेरेन पेडीकल स्क्रू बसयले. जखम (wound) नीट निवळ केली आनी थरा-थरांनी बंद केली. रक्ताचो जावपी नाश (estimated blood loss) उण्यांत उणो आशिल्लो. दुयेंतीन ही प्रक्रिया बरी सोसली. शस्त्रक्रिये उपरांतच्या येवजणेंत क्षयरोग विरोधी उपचार चालू दवरप आनी मधुमेहाचें वेवस्थापन करप हांचो आस्पाव आसा.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Siddappa Gorabaal]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-KT-2024-0815-001]]
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital, Kumta]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Basavaraj Gorabaal]] [[AGE|26]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Deshpande]]
Procedure / findings: The patient presented with a complex case of Pott's spine involving the thoracic vertebrae, a manifestation of extrapulmonary tuberculosis complicated by a non-communicable disease comorbidity of type 2 diabetes mellitus. Pre-operative workup included a CT scan showing a large paravertebral abscess and vertebral body destruction at T8-T9. The patient was placed under general anesthesia. A posterior midline approach was taken. Intra-operatively, significant granulation tissue and caseating material were encountered. The abscess was drained and debrided. The T8 and T9 vertebral bodies were partially resected to achieve decompression of the spinal cord. A titanium mesh cage was inserted to provide structural support, and pedicle screws were placed from T7 to T10 for stabilization. The wound was irrigated thoroughly and closed in layers. Estimated blood loss was minimal. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-operative plan includes continuation of anti-tubercular therapy and management of diabetes.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Siddappa Gorabaal]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-KT-2024-0815-001]]

persona: Basavaraj Gorabaal is a disciplined bus driver who balances precise route‑planning with lively Konkani bhajan humming, but has a habit of obsessively checking the bus’s fuel gauge and occasionally over‑engineering fixes with duct‑tape.
cultural_background: Basavaraj hails from a rural coastal community in Uttara Kannada, Karnataka, where Konkani culture blends with the broader Kannada milieu. His family follows traditional Hindu customs, celebrates coastal festivals like Ganesh Chaturthi and Ugadi, and participates in folk arts such as Yakshagana and Konkani bhajans. The daily rhythm revolves around agriculture, fishing, and the bus routes that connect villages to towns, reflecting a pragmatic, community‑oriented way of life.
linguistic_background: Basavaraj is fluent in Konkani, his mother tongue, and uses the Kannada script for reading and writing it. He is also fully proficient in Kannada, the state language, both in spoken and written form, which enables him to communicate with locals and manage official paperwork. His education up to Higher Secondary gave him functional proficiency in English, allowing him to understand road signs, fill out transport logs, and interact with passengers from diverse backgrounds. Overall, he reads and writes comfortably in Konkani (Kannada script), Kannada (Kannada script), and English (Latin script).
skills_and_expertise: Basavaraj possesses solid bus‑driving expertise, including safe handling of a heavy passenger vehicle on varied rural and highway roads. He is skilled in route planning, punctual schedule adherence, and passenger management, ensuring a calm and orderly journey. His practical knowledge extends to routine vehicle maintenance, basic mechanical troubleshooting, and emergency response. He demonstrates strong interpersonal communication in Konkani, Kannada and English, and employs disciplined record‑keeping for logs, fares, and safety checks. His organized mindset aids in optimizing fuel efficiency and managing daily operational challenges.

hobbies_and_interests: Basavaraj enjoys participating in village cricket matches and listening to Konkani folk songs that remind him of coastal celebrations. He takes part in Yakshagana performances during festivals, both as a backstage helper and occasional performer. In his free time, he likes tinkering with his bus engine using simple mutta (jugaad) solutions, reading devotional literature, and exploring nearby towns on off‑duty routes. Socialising with fellow drivers over tea, cooking traditional coastal dishes, and attending local fairs also feature prominently in his leisure.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Playing cricket (village team)", "Listening to Konkani folk music", "Participating in Yakshagana performances", "Vehicle tinkering (jugaad)", "Reading devotional literature", "Exploring nearby towns on off‑duty routes", "Socialising with fellow drivers over tea", "Cooking coastal dishes"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Basavaraj aims to upgrade his professional credentials by completing a certified Advanced Driver Training program and obtaining a Commercial Passenger Vehicle licence. He aspires to become a senior bus driver, responsible for training junior conductors and overseeing route safety. In the longer term, he wishes to manage a small fleet of intra‑district buses or open his own transport service, providing reliable connectivity for rural villages. His goals reflect his desire for structured advancement, higher earnings to support his family, and contributing to safer, more punctual public transport in his region.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

travel_persona: Zarifa Bibi prefers purposeful travel, often trekking the Pir Panjal hills or visiting the historic bazaars of Srinagar, plans occasional trips to Delhi for political meetings, and dreams of a cultural tour of Ladakh, but balances these trips with family duties and a modest travel budget.
culinary_persona: Zarifa Bibi enjoys cooking traditional Kashmiri dishes like Rogan Josh, Haak, and Gushtaba for her family, relishes the aroma of saffron and dried apricots, but typically relies on a local dhaba for weekday meals, and she’s adept at selecting quality Kashmiri spices from the market.
persona: Zarifa Bibi is a pragmatic yet curiosity‑driven political strategist who juggles a love for Sufi poetry and trekking with occasional disorganization and a skeptical edge, often forgetting bill payments while fiercely pursuing women’s empowerment.
cultural_background: Zarifa's cultural background is steeped in the rural Kashmiri Muslim traditions of Shupiyan, a small agrarian village in the Pulwama district of Jammu & Kashmir. She grew up in a close‑knit joint family that observes modest Kashmiri Hanafi customs, celebrates Eid and local harvest festivals, and cherishes the Sufi poetic heritage of poets like Lal‑Din and Habba Khatoon. The village’s simple lifestyle, emphasis on hospitality, and reliance on hand‑loom crafts and apple orchards have shaped her pragmatic outlook while her curiosity is fed by the region’s rich oral storytelling.
linguistic_background: Zarifa is a native speaker of Kashmiri, with full proficiency in reading, writing and speaking the language. She is fluent in Urdu, using it for literary pursuits, community outreach and official correspondence, and has an advanced command of English acquired through her teaching degree and political work. She also reads Hindi at a functional level for government forms and occasional media consumption.
skills_and_expertise: Zarifa combines over ten years of experience in senior political party organisation with a solid foundation in teaching. She excels in grassroots mobilisation, campaign strategy, policy advocacy and voter outreach, employing bilingual communication in Kashmiri, Urdu and English. Her expertise includes curriculum design, adult education, public speaking, conflict mediation, digital media management, data‑driven analysis, and organisational leadership. Her curiosity drives innovative problem‑solving, while her pragmatic side ensures realistic implementation of ideas.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Ramashish Kumar is an aspiring agricultural technology trainee who blends hands‑on farm expertise with a pragmatic, structured mindset, and they aim to modernise their family’s fields and eventually run a small dairy‑horticulture venture.
linguistic_persona: Ramashish Kumar speaks Maithili as his mother‑tongue, is fluent in conversational Hindi, and has a functional grasp of basic English for everyday interactions, and they often use their smartphone for quick translations.
sports_persona: Ramashish Kumar is an avid kabaddi player who trains with the Purnia Kabaddi Club and cheers for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, and they also follow Indian cricket fervently, especially the performances of batsman Virat Kohli.
arts_persona: Ramashish Kumar enjoys drawing traditional Mithila motifs such as peacock and fish during festivals, listens to folk singer Sharda Sinha, plays the dholak for community songs, and reads Hindi comic series like Chacha Chaudhary, which they consider a creative escape.
travel_persona: Ramashish Kumar prefers budget train journeys, having visited nearby Darbhanga and the pilgrimage site of Maa Baidyanath, and they dream of exploring Delhi’s museums while keeping travel expenses modest due to family constraints.
culinary_persona: Ramashish Kumar relishes traditional dishes like litti‑chokha, sattu‑paratha and makhana kheer, enjoys the sharp tang of mustard oil and the aroma of garlic, and they often help their mother prepare these meals during festivals, though they themselves seldom cook daily.
persona: Ramashish Kumar is a grounded village youth who balances farm duties with dreams of modern agriculture, loves folk music and dholak beats, saves cash under the mattress, and can be stubborn about skipping health check‑ups.
cultural_background: Ramashish belongs to the Maithili community of the Mithila cultural region, even though he lives in the rural outskirts of Purnia, Bihar. His family follows an agrarian lifestyle, with daily routines revolving around seasonal farming, community gatherings, and joint‑family solidarity. Traditional customs such as celebrating Chhath, Diwali, and the local harvest fair (Mela) are integral to his upbringing. The local cuisine of litti‑chokha, sattu, and makhana, as well as the practice of Mithila painting and folk songs, shape his everyday environment. Growing up in a village setting, he is accustomed to a rhythm that balances agricultural work, school, and communal celebrations.
linguistic_background: Ramashish's mother‑tongue is Maithili, which he reads and writes comfortably thanks to his primary schooling in the village school. He is fluent in spoken Hindi and can read simple texts, using it for official communication and daily interactions. His English proficiency is basic; he can understand and speak simple sentences, especially those learned from school and media, but his reading and writing skills are limited to everyday words and phrases. Overall, his literacy level aligns with a primary‑educated rural youth who relies on Maithili at home, Hindi in the community, and a functional grasp of English for occasional needs.
skills_and_expertise: Ramashish has practical skills acquired from his rural upbringing: assisting in farm work, managing household chores, basic animal care, simple arithmetic for market transactions, and competent use of a smartphone for communication and information. He also knows several Maithili folk songs, can prepare traditional dishes like litti‑chokha, and participates in village sports such as kabaddi. Additionally, he has basic first‑aid knowledge learned from community health camps.

hobbies_and_interests: Ramashish enjoys listening to Maithili folk music, playing the dholak, reading Hindi comic books, watching cricket matches on television, and playing kabaddi with friends in the village field. He also likes drawing simple Mithila art during festivals and helping his mother prepare traditional foods for celebrations.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Ramashish aspires to continue his education beyond primary school and enroll in a vocational training program focused on agricultural technology or basic electrical work. He aims to bring modern farming techniques to his family's fields, eventually managing his own small farm and possibly starting a dairy or horticulture venture. His long‑term ambition is to become a reliable breadwinner for his family while gaining respect as a knowledgeable and progressive member of his rural community.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Purnia.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Maithili (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Purnia
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 18, never married, retired homemaker, primary education, rural zone in Purnia, Bihar, speaks Maithili. Agrarian lifestyle with seasonal farming duties. Stubborn about health check-ups, saves cash at home. Aspiring agricultural technology trainee.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] DOB [[DOB|2006-04-15]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]]

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Kumar]] | Course / advice: Patient admitted with complaints of high-grade fever, chills, and generalized body ache for three days. On examination, he was febrile with temperature 102.4°F, pulse 110/min, BP 110/70 mmHg, and respiratory rate 20/min. Abdomen was soft, no tenderness. persona: Ramashish Kumar is a grounded village youth who balances farm duties with dreams of modern agriculture, loves folk music and dholak beats, saves cash under the mattress, and can be stubborn about skipping health check‑ups.
cultural_background: Ramashish belongs to the Maithili community of the Mithila cultural region, even though he lives in the rural outskirts of Purnia, Bihar. His family follows an agrarian lifestyle, with daily routines revolving around seasonal farming, community gatherings, and joint‑family solidarity. Traditional customs such as celebrating Chhath, Diwali, and the local harvest fair (Mela) are integral to his upbringing. The local cuisine of litti‑chokha, sattu, and makhana, as well as the practice of Mithila painting and folk songs, shape his everyday environment. Growing up in a village setting, he is accustomed to a rhythm that balances agricultural work, school, and communal celebrations.
linguistic_background: Ramashish's mother‑tongue is Maithili, which he reads and writes comfortably thanks to his primary schooling in the village school. He is fluent in spoken Hindi and can read simple texts, using it for official communication and daily interactions. His English proficiency is basic; he can understand and speak simple sentences, especially those learned from school and media, but his reading and writing skills are limited to everyday words and phrases. Overall, his literacy level aligns with a primary‑educated rural youth who relies on Maithili at home, Hindi in the community, and a functional grasp of English for occasional needs.
skills_and_expertise: Ramashish has practical skills acquired from his rural upbringing: assisting in farm work, managing household chores, basic animal care, simple arithmetic for market transactions, and competent use of a smartphone for communication and information. He also knows several Maithili folk songs, can prepare traditional dishes like litti‑chokha, and participates in village sports such as kabaddi. Additionally, he has basic first‑aid knowledge learned from community health camps.

hobbies_and_interests: Ramashish enjoys listening to Maithili folk music, playing the dholak, reading Hindi comic books, watching cricket matches on television, and playing kabaddi with friends in the village field. He also likes drawing simple Mithila art during festivals and helping his mother prepare traditional foods for celebrations.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Ramashish aspires to continue his education beyond primary school and enroll in a vocational training program focused on agricultural technology or basic electrical work. system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Purnia.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Maithili (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Purnia
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 18, never married, retired, primary education, rural, state of Bihar, district of Purnia, first language Maithili. He follows an agrarian lifestyle and is fluent in spoken Hindi. Clinically, he has a history of skipping health check-ups and saves cash at home. He is from the Maithili-speaking community in the Mithila region.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
रेफरल [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Purnia District Hospital]] सँ / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
विषय: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ram Prasad]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], जिला [[DISTRICT|Purnia]]
कारण: लगातार खाँसीक संग वजन कम होनाय आ रातिमे पसीना आनाय, जकरा लेल फुफ्फुसीय क्षय रोग (पल्मोनरी ट्यूबरकुलोसिस) क मूल्यांकन आ सह-रुग्ण गैर-संचारी रोगक आकलनक आवश्यकता अछि।

रोगी 18 वर्षक पुरुष छथि, जे बिहारक पूर्णिया जिलाक ग्रामीण पृष्ठभूमि सँ छथि, आ तीन सप्ताह सँ उत्पादक खाँसी (प्रोडक्टिव कफ), कम बुखार आ वजनमे उल्लेखनीय कमीक शिकायत कऽ रहल छथि। ओ कहैत छथि जे रातिमे पसीना अबैत अछि आ शारीरिक श्रम पर हल्का सांस फूलब (डिस्पनिया) होइत अछि। हुनकर महत्वपूर्ण संकेत (वाइटल्स) स्थिर अछि। छातीक एक्स-रे दाहिना ऊपरी लोबमे इन्फिल्ट्रेट्स देखबैत अछि, जे सक्रिय फुफ्फुसीय क्षय रोगक संकेत दैत अछि। हुनकर आयु आ लक्षणकेँ देखैत, कोनो अंतर्निहित गैर-संचारी रोगक पूर्ववृत्तिकेँ खारिज करबाक लेल एकटा व्यापक जाँचक आवश्यकता अछि।

रोगीक [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] अहाँक समीक्षाक लेल संलग्न अछि। कृपया कफक सूक्ष्मदर्शी जाँच (स्मीयर माइक्रोस्कोपी), जीनएक्सपर्ट (GeneXpert) MTB/RIF परीक्षण आ एकटा पूर्ण मेटाबॉलिक पैनलक व्यवस्था करू। हुनकर [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] अनुवर्ती (फॉलो-अप) लेल उपलब्ध अछि। कोनो प्रश्नक लेल, अहाँ हुनकर पिता, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Shyam Prasad]], सँ ओहि नंबर पर संपर्क कऽ सकैत छी।

हम अहाँक विशेषज्ञ राय आ प्रबंधन योजनाक अनुरोध करैत छी। हुनकर [[DRIVING_LICENCE|BR-09-2020-001234]] आ [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P9876543]] पहचानक उद्देश्य सँ फाइलमे अछि। हम अहाँक त्वरित ध्यानक प्रतीक्षा करैत छी।
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Purnia District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ram Prasad]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Purnia]]
Reason: Persistent cough with weight loss and night sweats, requiring evaluation for pulmonary tuberculosis and assessment of co-morbid non-communicable diseases.

Patient is an 18-year-old male from a rural background in Purnia district, Bihar, presenting with a three-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, and significant weight loss. He reports occasional night sweats and mild dyspnea on exertion. His vitals are stable. A chest X-ray shows infiltrates in the right upper lobe, suggestive of active pulmonary tuberculosis. Given his age and presentation, a comprehensive workup is needed to rule out any underlying non-communicable disease predisposition.

The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your review. Please arrange for sputum microscopy, GeneXpert MTB/RIF testing, and a full metabolic panel. His [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is available for follow-up. For any queries, you may contact his father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Shyam Prasad]], at the same number.

We request your expert opinion and management plan. His [[DRIVING_LICENCE|BR-09-2020-001234]] and [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P9876543]] are on file for identification purposes. We look forward to your prompt attention.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Purnia.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Devanagari script for Maithili (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Purnia
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 18, never married, retired homemaker, primary education, rural zone in Purnia district, Bihar, speaks Maithili as first language, and follows an agrarian lifestyle with limited health check-ups.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Jancy John, a 26‑year‑old homemaker with a flair for Kerala cuisine and meticulous budgeting, channels their love for structure into flawlessly organized church events and is now turning that disciplined energy into a fledgling home‑based catering venture.
linguistic_persona: Jancy John, fluent in Malayalam and equipped with functional English for reading notes, digital messaging, and basic online tasks, handles daily communication in their mother tongue while using English only when necessary, and has no formal training in Hindi.
sports_persona: Jancy John, who enjoys playing badminton at the local community centre and follows the Kerala Blasters in the Indian Super League, keeps fit through regular yoga sessions and weekend badminton matches, balancing competitive spirit with a relaxed approach to exercise.
arts_persona: Jancy John, an avid reader of Vaikom Muhammad Basheer and O.N.V. Kurup, delights in singing hymns with their church choir and unwinds by watching Mohanlal’s classic Malayalam films, weaving literary and cinematic influences into their everyday creativity.
travel_persona: Jancy John, who prefers well‑planned trips within a modest budget, often explores Kerala’s scenic spots such as Munnar, Alleppey’s backwaters, and the historic town of Fort Kochi, and dreams of a cultural pilgrimage to Delhi’s monuments and a short overseas getaway to Singapore, balancing family commitments with a love for gentle adventure.
culinary_persona: Jancy John, a skilled home chef specializing in traditional Kerala fare, excels at making fluffy appams, aromatic vegetable stew, and sweet payasam, frequently seasoning with fresh curry leaves, coconut milk, and tamarind, and delights in sourcing herbs from their kitchen garden to elevate family meals and church feasts.
persona: Jancy John, a 26‑year‑old meticulous homemaker who balances a love for structured budgeting with a secret obsession for spicy street snacks, turns everyday chores into mini‑projects and dreams of scaling their cherished Kerala recipes into a thriving catering venture.
cultural_background: Jancy John belongs to the Malayali Syrian Christian community in the rural outskirts of Thrissur, Kerala. Her family follows a blend of traditional Kerala customs—like Onam feasts, folk art, and agrarian rhythms—and Christian practices centered around the local parish, weekly Sunday worship, and communal celebrations of Christmas and Easter. The close‑knit village network provides a structured social fabric that aligns with her preference for predictability and organized routines.
linguistic_background: Jancy's first language is Malayalam, which she reads, writes and speaks fluently, handling household affairs, managing finances and communicating within her community. Her higher secondary education provided her with functional English; she can read simple texts, write basic notes, and understand spoken instructions, though she prefers Malayalam for most daily interactions. She has no formal training in Hindi or any other language.
skills_and_expertise: As a homemaker in a rural Kerala household, Jancy excels at managing day-to-day domestic operations. She is adept at cooking traditional Kerala cuisine, especially vegetarian fare for church feasts, and maintains meticulous household budgeting and expense tracking. Her organizational skills extend to coordinating church events and festivals, where she oversees logistics, menu planning, and volunteer scheduling. She possesses basic computer and smartphone proficiency for handling family finances, online shopping, and staying connected through messaging apps. Additionally, she tends to a modest kitchen garden, cultivating vegetables and herbs, reflecting her practical, hands‑on approach.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Malayalam household management', 'Kerala cuisine cooking', 'Budgeting and expense tracking', 'Church event coordination', 'Basic computer and smartphone usage', 'Gardening and small-scale farming', 'Volunteer management']
hobbies_and_interests: Jancy enjoys immersing herself in Malayalam literature, often reading novels by Vaikom Muhammad Basheer and poetry by O.N.V. Kurup. She finds relaxation in tending to her kitchen garden, growing vegetables like okra, ladyfinger and herbs such as curry leaves. Cooking traditional Kerala dishes—especially appams, stew, and payasam—for family and church events is a creative outlet for her. She participates in the church choir, singing hymns during services. In her leisure time, she watches Malayalam movies, plays badminton with friends, and practices basic yoga for health.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Malayalam literature', 'Gardening', 'Cooking traditional Kerala dishes', 'Church choir singing', 'Watching Malayalam movies', 'Playing badminton', 'Practicing yoga']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Jancy aspires to turn her passion for cooking and community service into a sustainable home‑based catering venture, initially focusing on church gatherings, weddings, and cultural events in Thrissur. She plans to enroll in a certificate program in nutrition or culinary arts to professionalise her skills and enhance the healthfulness of her menus. In the longer term, she hopes to become a coordinator for the local parish’s women’s ministry, organizing skill‑building workshops and outreach programs that empower other homemakers. These goals reflect her desire for structured growth, practical impact, and a balanced contribution to family and community.
persona_clinical_summary: A 26-year-old female homemaker from Thrissur, Kerala, currently married, with higher secondary education and Malayalam as her first language. She resides in a rural area and manages household finances meticulously while preparing traditional Kerala cuisine. Her lifestyle includes regular yoga and community event organization, reflecting a structured routine.

രോഗി: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Nair]] | ജനനത്തീയതി [[DOB|1998-07-22]] | പ്രായം: [[AGE|26]] | ലിംഗം: [[GENDER|Female]]
തൊഴിൽ: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | മെഡിക്കൽ റെക്കോർഡ് നമ്പർ: [[MRN|OPD-KL-TS-2024-089]] | ഡോക്ടർ: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sreekumar Menon]]
ബന്ധു: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Vijayakumar Nair]] | ഫോൺ: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
രജിസ്ട്രാർ എംപ്ലോയി ഐഡി: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-KL-TS-404]] | ജില്ല: [[DISTRICT|Thrissur]]
പ്രധാന പരാതി: 3 ദിവസമായി വലതുവശത്തെ അടിവയറ്റിൽ വേദന, പനി, ഛർദ്ദി.

നിലവിലെ രോഗചരിത്രം: രോഗി 26 വയസ്സുള്ള ഒരു ഗൃഹലക്ഷ്മിയാണ്, തൃശ്ശൂർ ജില്ലയിലെ ഒരു ഗ്രാമപ്രദേശത്തുനിന്നുള്ളവൾ. കഴിഞ്ഞ 3 ദിവസമായി വർദ്ധിച്ചുവരുന്നതും മൂർച്ചയുള്ളതുമായ വലതുവശത്തെ അടിവയറ്റിലെ വേദനയെക്കുറിച്ച് അവർ പരാതിപ്പെടുന്നു, ഇത് ആദ്യം പൊക്കിളിന് ചുറ്റും (periumbilical) ആയിരുന്നു, ഇപ്പോൾ കൃത്യമായി ഒരു സ്ഥലത്ത് മാത്രമായി ഒതുങ്ങിയിരിക്കുന്നു. കുറഞ്ഞ തോതിലുള്ള പനി (പരമാവധി 100.8°F), പിത്തരസം ഇല്ലാത്ത രണ്ട് തവണയുള്ള ഛർദ്ദി, വിശപ്പില്ലായ്മ എന്നിവയും ഇതിനോടൊപ്പമുണ്ട്. മലശോധന രീതിയിൽ മാറ്റങ്ങളൊന്നും റിപ്പോർട്ട് ചെയ്തിട്ടില്ല. മുൻപ് സമാനമായ എപ്പിസോഡുകൾ ഉണ്ടായിട്ടില്ല.

പരിശോധന: പരിശോധനയിൽ, രോഗിക്ക് പനി ഇല്ല (99.2°F), പൾസ് 98/മിനിറ്റ്, ബിപി 120/80 mmHg. അടിവയർ മൃദുവാണ്, വലതുവശത്തെ അടിവയറ്റിൽ കടുത്ത വേദനയും പേശികളുടെ മുറുക്കവും (guarding) കാണാം. റീബൗണ്ട് ടെൻഡർനസ് (Rebound tenderness) ഉണ്ട്. കുടലിലെ ശബ്ദങ്ങൾ (Bowel sounds) കുറവാണ്. ആന്തരിക അവയവങ്ങൾ വലുതായി (organomegaly) അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നില്ല.

പരിശോധനകൾ: കംപ്ലീറ്റ് ബ്ലഡ് കൗണ്ട് പരിശോധനയിൽ ന്യൂട്രോഫിലിക് പ്രിഡൊമിനൻസ് ഉള്ള ല്യൂക്കോസൈറ്റോസിസ് കാണപ്പെടുന്നു. സി-റിയാക്ടീവ് പ്രോട്ടീൻ (C-reactive protein) വർദ്ധിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ട്. വയറിന്റെ അൾട്രാസൗണ്ട് പരിശോധനയിൽ കട്ടിയുള്ള അപ്പെൻഡിക്സും അപ്പെൻഡിക്സിന് ചുറ്റുമുള്ള ദ്രാവക ശേഖരണവും കാണപ്പെടുന്നു.

രോഗനിർണ്ണയം: അക്യൂട്ട് അപ്പെൻഡിസൈറ്റിസ്, സങ്കീർണ്ണമായത്.

പദ്ധതി: 1. ശസ്ത്രക്രിയാ വിലയിരുത്തലിനായി പ്രവേശിപ്പിക്കുക. 2. ഇൻട്രാവീനസ് ഫ്ലൂയിഡുകളും ആന്റിബയോട്ടിക്കുകളും. 3. അപ്പെൻഡിസെക്ടമിക്ക് വേണ്ടിയുള്ള ശസ്ത്രക്രിയാ കൺസൾട്ടേഷൻ. 4. ശസ്ത്രക്രിയയെക്കുറിച്ചും ശസ്ത്രക്രിയയ്ക്ക് ശേഷമുള്ള പരിചരണത്തെക്കുറിച്ചും രോഗിയെയും ബന്ധുവിനെയും ഉപദേശിക്കുക.
generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Thrissur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-KL-TS-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Nair]] | DOB [[DOB|1998-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|26]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-KL-TS-2024-089]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sreekumar Menon]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Vijayakumar Nair]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-KL-TS-404]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Thrissur]]
Chief complaint: Right lower quadrant abdominal pain for 3 days with fever and vomiting.

History of present illness: Patient is a 26-year-old female homemaker from a rural area in Thrissur district. She reports a 3-day history of progressive, sharp, right lower quadrant abdominal pain, initially periumbilical and now localized. Associated symptoms include low-grade fever (max 100.8°F), two episodes of non-bilious vomiting, and decreased appetite. No change in bowel habits reported. No prior similar episodes.

Examination: On examination, the patient is afebrile (99.2°F), pulse 98/min, BP 120/80 mmHg. Abdomen is soft, with marked tenderness and guarding in the right lower quadrant. Rebound tenderness is present. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. No organomegaly palpable.

Investigations: Complete blood count shows leukocytosis with a neutrophilic predominance. C-reactive protein is elevated. Ultrasound abdomen shows a thickened appendix with peri-appendiceal fluid collection.

Diagnosis: Acute appendicitis, complicated.

Plan: 1. Admit for surgical evaluation. 2. Intravenous fluids and antibiotics. 3. Surgical consultation for appendectomy. 4. Counsel patient and relative regarding the procedure and post-operative care.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

GOOD:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

രോഗിക്ക് കഴിഞ്ഞ ആറ് മാസമായി ആവർത്തിച്ചുണ്ടാകുന്ന വായയിലെ വ്രണങ്ങളും കഠിനമായ ക്ഷീണവും അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നു. ജില്ലാ ആശുപത്രിയിലെ പ്രാഥമിക പരിശോധനകളിൽ വീക്കത്തിന്റെ സൂചകങ്ങൾ (inflammatory markers) ഉയർന്നതായി കാണപ്പെട്ടു. വായയിലെ ലൈക്കൺ പ്ലാനസ് (oral lichen planus) ആണെന്ന് പ്രാഥമികമായി രോഗനിർണ്ണയം നടത്തി. കുടുംബപരമായ ഉത്തരവാദിത്തങ്ങളും സമീപകാലത്തെ സാമ്പത്തിക ബുദ്ധിമുട്ടുകളും കാരണം രോഗിക്ക് വലിയ മാനസിക സമ്മർദ്ദമുണ്ടെന്ന് റിപ്പോർട്ട് ചെയ്തു. പരിശോധനയിൽ, വായയ്ക്കുള്ളിലെ വശങ്ങളിൽ ചുവന്ന പാടുകളും വിക്കംസ് സ്ട്രിയേ (Wickham's striae) എന്ന അവസ്ഥയും കണ്ടെത്തി. രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം 120/80 mmHg, നാഡിമിടിപ്പ് 78/മിനിറ്റ്, താപനില 98.4°F.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

sports_persona: Ayyamma Vaalikar avidly plays kabaddi with the village team, cheers for the Royal Challengers Bangalore in cricket, follows local football club FC Bengaluru United, and they organize spontaneous street cricket games during festivals, reflecting a competitive spirit and love for team camaraderie.
arts_persona: Ayyamma Vaalikar enjoys listening to Marathi bhavgeet and Kannada folk songs, sketches bustling village scenes on old notebook paper, admires street murals in nearby towns, and they follow the music of Lata Mangeshkar and the films of Rajkumar, letting these artistic influences fuel their creative side.
travel_persona: Ayyamma Vaalikar prefers short road trips to nearby hill stations like Mahabaleshwar and historic towns like Hampi, plans travel with friends on a modest budget, enjoys spontaneous train rides during festivals, and they dream of visiting Goa's beaches when finances allow.
culinary_persona: Ayyamma Vaalikar loves the tangy taste of Maharashtrian misal pav, the hearty texture of Karnataka's bisi bele bath, enjoys crunchy peanuts and spicy chivda at fairs, and they prefer homemade poha for breakfast, though they rarely cook themselves and often indulge in street food bazaars.
persona: Ayyamma Vaalikar blends vigilant rural security work with a love for folk music, spontaneous games, and modest savings, juggling curiosity and a laid‑back streak while dreaming of a supervisory role.
cultural_background: Ayyamma hails from the rural outskirts of Belgaum in Karnataka, a region where Marathi-speaking communities coexist with Kannada culture. Her family follows traditional agrarian customs, celebrates festivals like Ganesh Chaturthi and Ugadi, and maintains the strong communal bond typical of village life in the Deccan plateau.
linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Marathi, which she speaks fluently and understands well in both spoken and basic written form. She is also proficient in Kannada, allowing her to communicate effectively with neighbours and local authorities. Due to limited formal schooling (below primary), her literacy in Marathi and Kannada is functional but not advanced; she can read simple notices and fill out basic security logs. She has a passive understanding of Hindi but does not read or write it.
skills_and_expertise: Ayyamma has practical experience as a security officer in a rural setting. She is skilled in vigilant observation, patrolling, crowd control, and basic first aid. She can handle simple security equipment, maintain incident logs, and coordinate with local police when needed. Her multilingual ability allows her to communicate warnings and instructions in Marathi and Kannada effectively. Additionally, she possesses basic knowledge of village safety protocols and emergency response.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
