Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

முக்கிய அறிகுறிகள் / தீவிரத்தன்மை: நோயாளி விழிப்புடன் உள்ளார், கடுமையான வலியுடன் உள்ளார். இரத்த அழுத்தம் 140/90, இதயத் துடிப்பு 110, சுவாச வீதம் 24, அறை காற்றில் SpO2 96%. GCS 15. வகைப்படுத்துதல் வகை: சிவப்பு.

செவிலியர் குறிப்புகள்:
நோயாளி கட்டுமான தளத்தில் சுமார் 2 மணிநேரத்திற்கு முன்பு உயரத்திலிருந்து கீழே விழுந்த வரலாற்றைக் கொண்டு வந்தார். வலது கீழ் காலில் கடுமையான வலி இருப்பதையும், எடையைத் தாங்க இயலாமையையும் அவர் தெரிவிக்கிறார். பரிசோதனையில் வலது டிபியா (tibia) மற்றும் ஃபைபுலா (fibula) எலும்புகள் கடுமையாகச் சிதைந்துள்ளதும், கணுக்கால் எலும்பின் நடுப்பகுதியில் சுமார் 5x3 செ.மீ. அளவுள்ள திறந்த காயம் இருப்பதும், எலும்பு வெளியே தெரிவதையும் வெளிப்படுத்துகிறது. தீவிர இரத்தப்போக்கு ஏதும் காணப்படவில்லை. காலின் நுனிப்பகுதி நாடித்துடிப்புகள் உணரக்கூடியதாக இருந்தாலும் பலவீனமாக உள்ளன. லேசான தொடுதலுக்கு உணர்வு சீராக உள்ளது. ஆரம்பகட்ட மதிப்பீடு குஸ்டிலோ-ஆண்டர்சன் வகை II திறந்த டிபியல் தண்டு எலும்பு முறிவைக் குறிக்கிறது. நோயாளி NPO நிலையில் உள்ளார். இடது முன்கையில் 18G கேனுலா மூலம் IV அணுகல் ஏற்படுத்தப்பட்டது. வலி நிவாரணம் வழங்கப்பட்டது: மார்பின் (Morphine) 5 மி.கி. IV. டெட்டனஸ் தடுப்பு மருந்து வழங்கப்பட்டது. காயம் கிருமி நீக்கம் செய்யப்பட்ட உப்புநீர் நனைத்த காஸ் கொண்டு மூடப்பட்டது. எலும்பு சிகிச்சை குழுவிற்குத் தகவல் தெரிவிக்கப்பட்டது. அவசர அறுவை சிகிச்சை சுத்திகரிப்பு மற்றும் திறந்த குறைப்பு உள் நிலைநிறுத்தத்திற்கு (ORIF) நோயாளி தயார் செய்யப்பட்டார். உறவினரிடமிருந்து நடைமுறைக்கான ஒப்புதல் பெறப்பட்டது. நோயாளி அறுவை சிகிச்சை அறைக்கு (OT) மாற்றப்பட்டார்.

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Madurai Medical College Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Karthik]] [[AGE|29]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|03]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|TN59AB1234]].
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|R. Lakshmi]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. Ramesh]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient is conscious, alert, in severe pain. BP 140/90, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 96% on room air. GCS 15. Triage category: Red.

Nursing Notes:
Patient presented with a history of a fall from height at the construction site approximately 2 hours prior to arrival. He reports severe pain in the right lower leg with inability to bear weight. Examination reveals a grossly deformed right tibia and fibula with an open wound over the lateral aspect of the mid-calf, approximately 5x3 cm, with exposed bone. No active bleeding noted. Distal pulses are palpable but weak. Sensation is intact to light touch. Initial assessment suggests a Gustilo-Anderson Type II open tibial shaft fracture. Patient is NPO. IV access established with 18G cannula in the left forearm. Analgesia administered: Morphine 5mg IV. Tetanus prophylaxis given. Wound covered with sterile saline-soaked gauze. Orthopedic team notified. Patient prepared for emergency surgical debridement and open reduction internal fixation (ORIF). Consent for procedure obtained from relative. Patient transferred to OT.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Prakasam.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Telugu script for Telugu (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Puncher, Metal
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Prakasam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32-year-old male metal puncher from Prakasam district, Andhra Pradesh, who is currently married, has an illiterate education, and speaks Telugu as his first language. He works in a rural setting and follows a routine that includes morning walks and folk songs. His lifestyle is characterized by a focus on work, family savings, and occasional back strain.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ప్రక్రియ / ఫలితాలు: రోగి గత మూడు రోజులుగా తీవ్రమైన ఆందోళన మరియు కుటుంబ సభ్యుల పట్ల హింసాత్మక ప్రవర్తనతో వచ్చారు. ప్రాథమిక అంచనా తీవ్రమైన ఆందోళన, నిద్రలేమి మరియు శ్రవణ భ్రాంతులను (auditory hallucinations) వెల్లడించింది. రోగి తనకే మరియు ఇతరులకు ప్రమాదకరమని భావించబడింది, కాబట్టి అత్యవసర జోక్యం అవసరమైంది. అతని భార్య [[RELATIVE_NAME|Lakshmi]] నుండి సమాచారంతో కూడిన సమ్మతి పొందిన తర్వాత, రోగి ఎలక్ట్రోకన్వల్సివ్ థెరపీ (ECT) కోసం సిద్ధం చేయబడ్డారు. సాధారణ అనస్థీషియా కింద ప్రక్రియ నిర్వహించబడింది మరియు సుమారు 45 సెకన్ల పాటు మూర్ఛ (seizure) ప్రేరేపించబడింది. ప్రక్రియ తర్వాత, రోగిని రికవరీ ఏరియాలో రెండు గంటల పాటు పర్యవేక్షించారు, ఆపై వార్డుకు తిరిగి పంపబడ్డారు. ECT తర్వాత రోగి మానసిక స్థితి గందరగోళంగా ఉన్నప్పటికీ స్థిరంగా ఉంది. ప్రతికూల సంఘటనలు ఏవీ గమనించబడలేదు. రోగి భార్యకు చికిత్స ప్రణాళిక మరియు తదుపరి షెడ్యూల్ గురించి కౌన్సెలింగ్ ఇవ్వబడింది. రోగి యొక్క [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] ప్రక్రియ వివరాలతో నవీకరించబడింది. పరిశీలన కోసం అతనికి [[BED_NUMBER|B2-08]] కేటాయించబడింది. అతని [[PATIENT_ID|PID-PRK-2024-0032]] ఈ ఎన్‌కౌంటర్ [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-1123-01]] తో అనుసంధానించబడింది. రోగికి వారానికి మూడు సార్లు ఆరు ECT సెషన్ల కోర్సు షెడ్యూల్ చేయబడింది. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-AP-045]] ప్రోటోకాల్ ECT అనుసరించబడింది. తదుపరి కమ్యూనికేషన్ కోసం రోగి యొక్క [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] రికార్డ్ చేయబడింది. రోగి [[VILLAGE|Chandrasekharapuram]], [[DISTRICT|Prakasam]] లో నివసిస్తున్నారు.

Procedure / findings: The patient presented with a history of acute agitation and violent behavior towards family members over the past three days. Initial assessment revealed severe anxiety, insomnia, and auditory hallucinations. The patient was deemed a danger to self and others, necessitating urgent intervention. After obtaining informed consent from his wife, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Lakshmi]], the patient was prepared for electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). The procedure was performed under general anesthesia with a brief seizure induced, lasting approximately 45 seconds. Post-procedure, the patient was monitored in the recovery area for two hours and then transferred back to the ward. The patient's immediate post-ECT mental status was confused but stabilized. The patient was observed for any adverse events, which were not noted. The patient's wife was counseled on the treatment plan and follow-up schedule. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] was updated with the procedure details. He was assigned [[BED_NUMBER|B2-08]] for observation. His [[PATIENT_ID|PID-PRK-2024-0032]] is linked to this encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-1123-01]]. The patient is scheduled for a course of six ECT sessions, three times a week. The [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-AP-045]] protocol for ECT was followed. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] was recorded for follow-up communication. The patient resides in [[VILLAGE|Chandrasekharapuram]], [[DISTRICT|Prakasam]].

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Prakasam.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Telugu script for Telugu (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Puncher, Metal
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Prakasam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32‑year‑old male metal puncher from Prakasam district, Andhra Pradesh, who is currently married and has no formal education. He speaks Telugu as his first language and works in a rural workshop, where he operates punching presses and performs routine maintenance. His lifestyle includes daily sunrise walks, folk music, and saving for his children, while he manages occasional back strain. He prefers quiet environments and avoids crowds.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

travel_persona: Mohan Narayanan prefers pilgrimages to sacred Vaishnavite sites like Tirupati and Venkateswara, enjoys weekend getaways to Ooty’s tea estates, and dreams of exploring Delhi’s historic monuments to connect with his Urdu roots, and they plan trips with careful budgeting and a modest travel itinerary.
culinary_persona: Mohan Narayanan loves the aromatic tang of sambar, the crispness of idli, and the fragrant layers of biryani, enjoys savoring mango pickles and coriander chutney, but rarely cooks for himself, preferring to order street‑food chaat on busy evenings, and they occasionally experiment with homemade chutney for the pickle business.
persona: Mohan Narayanan blends Urdu poetry with Tamil traditions, budgets meticulously for his pickle dreams, and despite a reserved nature, they chat about cricket scores over evening tea.
cultural_background: Mohan grew up in the bustling urban neighbourhood of Salem, Tamil Nadu, where the streets echo with Tamil chatter, but his home resonated with Urdu poetry and North‑Indian flavours. As a Hindu family that migrated from the Uttar‑Pradesh region in the early 1970s, his cultural tapestry weaves together Tamil festivals like Pongal with Urdu literary gatherings, creating a hybrid identity that respects both the Tamil and Urdu heritage while rooted firmly in Hindu rituals.
linguistic_background: Mohan’s mother tongue is Urdu, which he reads and writes fluently thanks to early exposure at home and community gatherings. He acquired Tamil as his second language during school, achieving functional literacy and comfortable conversational skills that allow him to navigate daily life in Salem. His formal education ended at the secondary level, so his English proficiency is modest, limited to basic reading of newspapers and occasional correspondence. He is confident in both Urdu and Tamil scripts, and can switch seamlessly between languages in social settings.
skills_and_expertise: Mohan possesses practical household management skills honed over years of running his family home. He is adept at budgeting, planning daily chores, and coordinating family events. His culinary expertise spans traditional Tamil dishes such as idli, sambar, and biryani influenced by North‑Indian flavours. He maintains a modest vegetable garden, applying basic horticultural techniques. He is digitally literate, comfortable using a smartphone for WhatsApp, video calls, and online banking. His Urdu proficiency enables him to read classical poetry and translate religious verses. He also volunteers occasionally as a coordinator for temple festivals, showcasing organizational and leadership abilities.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household Management", "Budgeting & Financial Planning", "Cooking (Tamil & North Indian Cuisine)", "Gardening", "Basic Digital Literacy", "Urdu Poetry Reading and Translation", "Temple Festival Coordination", "Basic First Aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Mohan enjoys immersing himself in Urdu poetry, often reciting verses of Ghalib and Faiz in his quiet study. He is an avid listener of Carnatic classical music and occasionally plays the harmonium during family gatherings. Gardening provides him solace, and he tends to a small balcony garden of herbs and seasonal vegetables. He loves watching classic Tamil cinema, especially films of the golden era starring Sivaji Ganesan. Board games like chess and card games such as rummy keep his mind sharp. He also practices calligraphy, writing Urdu couplets in elegant script.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reciting Urdu Poetry", "Listening to Carnatic Music", "Playing Harmonium", "Gardening (herbs & seasonal vegetables)", "Watching Classic Tamil Cinema", "Playing Chess", "Playing Rummy", "Practicing Calligraphy"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired from formal employment, Mohan aspires to deepen his involvement in community service. He aims to mentor youth in his neighbourhood on financial prudence and cultural literacy, especially bridging Urdu and Tamil heritage. He wishes to start a small home‑based venture producing traditional pickles and chutneys, leveraging his culinary skills and local market demand. He also plans to undertake a pilgrimage to key Vaishnavite sites such as Tirupati and Venkateswara Temple, combining spiritual fulfillment with personal health goals. Additionally, he intends to improve his Tamil fluency to assist his grandchildren with schoolwork.

He resides in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as his first language, with Tamil as a second language. His lifestyle includes meticulous budgeting and a preference for low-impact activities like morning walks.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Salem.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Arabic script for Urdu (not English; not a different Indic script).

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 48
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Salem
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Mohan Narayanan, a 48-year-old retired male from Salem, Tamil Nadu, is currently married and has a matriculation education. He resides in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as his first language, with Tamil as a second language. His lifestyle includes meticulous budgeting and a preference for low-impact activities like morning walks.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mohan Narayanan]]: آپ کا آنکولوجی فالو اپ [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-ONC-240521-01]] ہے [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Salem]] میں 21-مئی کو صبح 10:30 بجے [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]] کے ساتھ۔ براہ کرم [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] کے ذریعے تصدیق کریں۔ MRN [[MRN|MRN-TN-2024-0815]]۔
generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mohan Narayanan]]: Your oncology follow-up is [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-ONC-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Salem]] on 21-May at 10:30 AM with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]. Please confirm via [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-TN-2024-0815]].
judge_reasoning: Urdu Arabic-script prose matches expectation; all TYPEs allowed; Latin tag values per rules; oncology SMS fits male/48/Salem persona with no mismatches or length issues.

مریض نے مستقل طور پر کم موڈ، اینہیڈونیا (anhedonia)، اور نیند کی خرابی کے ساتھ تین ماہ سے پیش کیا ہے۔ وہ خود کو بے وقعت محسوس کرنے اور روزمرہ کی سرگرمیوں پر توجہ مرکوز کرنے میں دشواری کی اطلاع دیتا ہے۔ سابقہ نفسیاتی تاریخ منفی ہے۔ منشیات کے استعمال کی کوئی تاریخ نہیں ہے۔ خاندانی تاریخ غیر متعلقہ ہے۔ معائنے پر، مریض ہوش میں ہے اور سمت یافتہ ہے۔ نفسیاتی حرکات میں سستی (psychomotor retardation) ہے۔ موڈ افسردہ ہے، اثرات مطابقت رکھتے ہیں۔ خودکشی کے خیالات نوٹ نہیں کیے گئے۔ تشخیص: میجر ڈپریسیو ڈس آرڈر، معتدل شدت۔ علاج کے منصوبے میں سیروٹونن 50 ملی گرام روزانہ شروع کرنا اور فالو اپ کے لیے نفسیاتی آؤٹ پیشنٹ کلینک میں ریفرل شامل ہے۔ مریض کو نیند کی صفائی اور باقاعدہ ورزش کے بارے میں مشورہ دیا گیا۔

Patient presented with chief complaint of persistent low mood, anhedonia, and sleep disturbances for the past three months. He reports feelings of worthlessness and difficulty concentrating on daily activities. Past psychiatric history is negative. There is no history of substance abuse. Family history is non-contributory. On examination, the patient is alert and oriented, with psychomotor retardation. Mood is depressed, affect is congruent. No suicidal ideation noted. Diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, moderate severity. Treatment plan includes initiation of Sertraline 50 mg daily and referral to the psychiatry outpatient clinic for follow-up. Patient advised on sleep hygiene and regular exercise.

professional_persona: Mohan Narayanan, a retired household manager, channels his meticulous budgeting, event coordination, and culinary expertise into mentoring neighbourhood youth on financial prudence and cultural literacy, and they plan a modest home‑based pickle venture with careful notebooks.
linguistic_persona: Mohan Narayanan fluently reads and writes Urdu, converses comfortably in Tamil, and handles basic English newspaper reading and digital communication, and they seamlessly switch scripts to connect with their community.
sports_persona: Mohan Narayanan enjoys watching cricket, especially cheering for the Chennai Super Kings, plays casual chess and rummy with friends, and goes for early morning walks around his neighbourhood park to stay fit, and they prefer low‑impact activities over high‑intensity sports.
arts_persona: Mohan Narayanan recites Ghalib and Faiz at family gatherings, listens avidly to Carnatic ragas, occasionally plays the harmonium, admires classic Tamil cinema icons like Sivaji Ganesan, and they practice Urdu calligraphy as a meditative art.
travel_persona: Mohan Narayanan prefers pilgrimages to sacred Vaishnavite sites like Tirupati and Venkateswara, enjoys weekend getaways to Ooty’s tea estates, and dreams of exploring Delhi’s historic monuments to connect with his Urdu roots, and they plan trips with careful budgeting and a modest travel itinerary.
culinary_persona: Mohan Narayanan loves the aromatic tang of sambar, the crispness of idli, and the fragrant layers of biryani, enjoys savoring mango pickles and coriander chutney, but rarely cooks for himself, preferring to order street‑food chaat on busy evenings, and they occasionally experiment with homemade chutney for the pickle business.
persona: Mohan Narayanan blends Urdu poetry with Tamil traditions, budgets meticulously for his pickle dreams, and despite a reserved nature, they chat about cricket scores over evening tea.
cultural_background: Mohan grew up in the bustling urban neighbourhood of Salem, Tamil Nadu, where the streets echo with Tamil chatter, but his home resonated with Urdu poetry and North‑Indian flavours. As a Hindu family that migrated from the Uttar‑Pradesh region in the early 1970s, his cultural tapestry weaves together Tamil festivals like Pongal with Urdu literary gatherings, creating a hybrid identity that respects both the Tamil and Urdu heritage while rooted firmly in Hindu rituals.
linguistic_background: Mohan’s mother tongue is Urdu, which he reads and writes fluently thanks to early exposure at home and community gatherings. He acquired Tamil as his second language during school, achieving functional literacy and comfortable conversational skills that allow him to navigate daily life in Salem. His formal education ended at the secondary level, so his English proficiency is modest, limited to basic reading of newspapers and occasional correspondence. He is confident in both Urdu and Tamil scripts, and can switch seamlessly between languages in social settings.
skills_and_expertise: Mohan possesses practical household management skills honed over years of running his family home. He is adept at budgeting, planning daily chores, and coordinating family events. His culinary expertise spans traditional Tamil dishes such as idli, sambar, and biryani influenced by North‑Indian flavours. He maintains a modest vegetable garden, applying basic horticultural techniques. He is digitally literate, comfortable using a smartphone for WhatsApp, video calls, and online banking. His Urdu proficiency enables him to read classical poetry and translate religious verses. He also volunteers occasionally as a coordinator for temple festivals, showcasing organizational and leadership abilities.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household Management", "Budgeting & Financial Planning", "Cooking (Tamil & North Indian Cuisine)", "Gardening", "Basic Digital Literacy", "Urdu Poetry Reading and Translation", "Temple Festival Coordination", "Basic First Aid"]
hobbies_and_interests: Mohan enjoys immersing himself in Urdu poetry, often reciting verses of Ghalib and Faiz in his quiet study. He is an avid listener of Carnatic classical music and occasionally plays the harmonium during family gatherings. Gardening provides him solace, and he tends to a small balcony garden of herbs and seasonal vegetables. He loves watching classic Tamil cinema, especially films of the golden era starring Sivaji Ganesan. Board games like chess and card games such as rummy keep his mind sharp. He also practices calligraphy, writing Urdu couplets in elegant script.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reciting Urdu Poetry", "Listening to Carnatic Music", "Playing Harmonium", "Gardening (herbs & seasonal vegetables)", "Watching Classic Tamil Cinema", "Playing Chess", "Playing Rummy", "Practicing Calligraphy"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired from formal employment, Mohan aspires to deepen his involvement in community service. He aims to mentor youth in his neighbourhood on financial prudence and cultural literacy, especially bridging Urdu and Tamil heritage. He wishes to start a small home‑based venture producing traditional pickles and chutneys, leveraging his culinary skills and local market demand. He also plans to undertake a pilgrimage to key Vaishnavite sites such as Tirupati and Venkateswara Temple, combining spiritual fulfillment with personal health goals. Additionally, he intends to improve his Tamil fluency to assist his grandchildren with schoolwork.

His first language is Urdu, and he manages household finances meticulously while engaging in cultural activities like reciting poetry and listening to Carnatic music. He prefers low-impact exercise such as morning walks and avoids high-risk sports.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
